title: Living in Boulder vlog 6 - 15 - 2017: Topography of Rome

enrichment.summary: Kyle records a late-night vlog from Boulder giving casual life updates — helping his mom, going on a ride, being behind on his site. The bulk of the video covers his current reading project: working through a stack of classical reference works on ancient Rome (Platner & Ashby's Topographical Dictionary, Mommsen, and Gibbon) to fuel a Google Earth reconstruction of the city, noting that only ~10% of the Forma Urbis Romae marble plan survives, leaving gaps he wants to fill with the dictionary.
enrichment.speaker: kyle

stances.position: wants to make it as realistic as possible by using classical sources to fill gaps left by the incomplete Forma Urbis Romae
acknowledges slow progress because the big reference volumes take days per entry
wants to get back to the Google Earth project after finishing the Topographical Dictionary

entities: Decline and Fall of the Roman Empire

transcript.text: Hello, Living in Boulder Vlog for, um, right now it's 1.05 a.m. in June 2016, so I'll say like June 2015.
So, um, uh, yeah, I got too lazy, I guess, to do one yesterday.
Um, I worked pretty late yesterday, and I did something, oh yeah, my mom got a tricep a little bit, and we went on a ride.
And then today, um, I've been kind of busy, so I haven't been doing that well in, you know, progress on my site, but, um, today I went over and, uh, helped my mom with some stuff, vacuuming.
And, um, and, um, so, I can't remember what book I said, I think it was Clement of Alexander, um, that I was doing last time, and then since then I did, uh, maybe a couple more.
Um, but right now I'm doing, um, Platner and Ashby, Topographical Dictionary of Ancient Rome, which was written in 1920.
I'm doing all of the classics right now, um, um, like everything from until the end of the Roman Empire, um, but, uh, I have some others too, just a couple others.

I have, uh, Momsen, um, which was written in, like, 1780, about Rome, and then, uh, Given, which is 18, 8,000 pages written in the 1860s, 50s, and then, uh, and then, so now I'm getting this one, um, which is 1920, Topography Rome.
So, because I'm, uh, building Rome on Google Earth, I'd like to get going on that project, like, after this one.
But, um, um, um, there's lots of, there are only 10% of the Tabla Servia, Plant of Servia, this big plant, um, map of the city that was 30 feet high,
That was on the side of a, of a temple.
Uh, only 10 of it is, is left, so when you look at the, um, at the map, there's big splotches, so it's just empty.
So, I can use this book to, uh, fill in those gaps, uh, to make it as realistic as possible.
Um, so, uh, but, but, like, this, uh, classic section in Rome,
Just the two of them can very well gobble up the rest of the year.
Um, I'm also a Googler in Boulder, so there's a few things that I really want to do, but, um,
I was, uh, trying to tell my mom today that it might be the end of 2018.
So, before I, you know, I'm done, like, working all the time on the website, she didn't like it, I didn't know much.
Um, so I'm going to try and sell my car to get money, um, which I'll do that soon.
I've got to do a video of it, but, um, I've been addicted to my sight doing these books.
I was, I was doing a pretty good pace, not having ones off, but then I came across this one.
The problem is, you know, it's hard to guess how long it's going to take,
Because you come across these really books, these really big books that take, like, you know, three days just to do one book.
And this one, like,
